recipe.name: Acetic acid
recipe.workup: Add concentrated acid slowly into 0.8 vol water
recipe.note: Work inside a certified chemical fume hood

recipe.name: Adenosine triphosphate
recipe.note: The exact stoichiometry of the hydrate is unknown, the monohydrate is commonly used as reference

recipe.name: Amphotericin B
recipe.note: Amphotericin B is insoluble in water
Add at 2.5 to 10 microgram per milliliter to tissue culture medium to prevent or treat yeast and other fungus contamination

safety.hazard: Liver and kidney toxicity upon repeated exposure

recipe.name: Ampicillin
recipe.note: Working concentration is 20 to 100 microgram per milliliter
Activity is lost when used above 7.0; consider carbenicillin for long-term growth

recipe.name: Betaine
recipe.note: The powder dissolves slowly and may not go fully in solution unless autoclaved

recipe.name: Blasticidin S
recipe.note: Working concentration is 50 to 100 microgram per milliliter for yeast; 2 to 10 microgram per milliliter for mammalian cell selection
The pH of the aequous solution should not exceed 7.0 to prevent inactivation of Blasticidin
On thawing, use what you need and discard the unusused portion after two weeks

safety.hazard: Liver and kidney toxicity upon repeated exposure

recipe.name: Bovine serum albumin
recipe.workup: Filter sterilize with very low protein-binding filter (CA, PES, RC)
recipe.note: Choose BSA grade according to needs; some products must be heat-treated to inactivate residual nuclease activity

recipe.name: Buffer P2 (Lysis buffer)
recipe.note: Thymolphthalein serves as pH indicator to monitor the neutralization reaction with N3; it is optional

recipe.name: Buffer P3 (Neutralization buffer)
recipe.note: For use with anion-exchange columns as in the QIAGEN Plasmid Kit

recipe.name: Buffer N3 (Neutralization buffer)
recipe.note: For use with silica-gel membranes as in the QIAGEN QIAprep Spin Miniprep Kit

recipe.name: Buffer PB (Binding buffer)
recipe.workup: Adjust pH with acetic acid before adding isopropyl alcohol

safety.hazard: Central nervous system toxicity upon single exposure
Central nervous system toxicity upon single exposure
Heart, liver, kidney, blood toxicity upon repeated exposure

recipe.name: Carbenicillin
recipe.note: Working concentration is 20 to 100 microgram per milliliter

recipe.name: Chloramphenicol
recipe.note: Photochemical decomposition results in yellowing
Working concentration is 10 to 30 microgram per milliliter

recipe.name: Ciprofloxacin
recipe.note: Ciprofloxacin can be added at 10 to 20 microgram per milliliter to tissue culture medium to prevent or treat mycoplasma contamination

recipe.name: Dithiothreitol
recipe.note: Dithiothreitol is less volatile than 2-mercaptoethanol and forms a thermodynamically stable ring upon disulfide reduction, which is why dithiothreitol is effective at lower concentrations than 2-mercaptoethanol

recipe.name: Doxycycline
recipe.workup: Mild warming may be required to fully dissolve the material
recipe.note: Working concentration is 0.1 to 10 microgram per milliliter in cell culture
Doxycycline is a broad spectrum inhibitor of matrix metalloproteases in vivo

recipe.name: Ethidium bromide
recipe.note: The powder is fatal if inhaled; work in a certified chemical fume hood

safety.disposal: Inactivate liquid waste over activated carbon matrix
Dispose empty containers as HAZARDOUS WASTE

recipe.name: Ethidium bromide
recipe.note: Working range for staining double-stranded DNA or RNA with low background is 0.2 to 0.5 milligram per liter; allow 20 minute to complete

safety.disposal: Inactivate liquid waste over activated carbon matrix
Solutions and gels with less than 100 microgram per milliliter ethidium bromide can be swathed in cling wrap and trashed

recipe.name: Ethylenediaminetetraacetate
recipe.workup: Stir vigorously; EDTA will not dissolve well until 7

recipe.name: Ficoll® 400
recipe.workup: Add slowly under constant stirring to 20 milliliter water; make up to the final volume when dissolved

recipe.name: Geneticin
recipe.note: Working concentration is 50 to 200 microgram per milliliter for yeast
Geneticin is not effective to select yeast in minimal media containing ammonium sulfate; supplement 1 gram per liter monosodium glutamate

recipe.name: Glucose
recipe.note: This stock is 1.1 molar

recipe.name: Glycerol
recipe.note: Pure glycerol has a density of 1.26 gram per milliliter at 20 celsius
This stock is 6.8 molar

safety.precaution: Reacts heavily with strong oxidizers such as permanganates and peroxides

recipe.name: Guanidine hydrochloride
recipe.workup: If precipitation is present, warm to 37 celsius and mix until completely dissolved prior to use

recipe.name: HEPES
recipe.workup: Filter sterilize through a 0.45 micrometer nitrocellulose filter, PVDF membranes will clog
recipe.note: Use between pH 6.8 to 8.2 unitless as buffer

recipe.name: Hydrochloric acid
recipe.note: Solution has 0.0
Work inside a chemical fume hood
Add concentrated acid slowly into 0.8 vol water

recipe.name: Hygromycin B
recipe.note: Working concentration is 200 to 300 microgram per milliliter for yeast

recipe.name: Imidazole
recipe.note: Use between pH 6.2 to 7.8 unitless as buffer

recipe.name: Kanamycin
recipe.note: Working concentration is 50 microgram per milliliter

recipe.name: LB medium (Miller)
recipe.note: Although the pH is adjusted near 7.0 with sodium hydroxide, LB medium is not very highly buffered

recipe.name: Magnesium chloride
recipe.note: The solid is very hygroscopic; buy only small quantities

recipe.name: Minimal growth medium
recipe.note: Basal Medium Eagle Vitamins are purchased from Life Science Technology

recipe.name: 2-(N-Morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid
recipe.note: Use between pH 5.5 to 6.7 unitless as buffer

recipe.name: Nourseothricin
recipe.note: Working concentration is 100 microgram per milliliter for yeast
Purchase from WERNER BioAgents GmbH, Jena (Germany)

recipe.name: Phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride
recipe.workup: Stir viguorously if added to a solution without detergents
recipe.note: Make fresh dilutions from stock for each use
Half-life in aqueous solution is less than 30 minute at room temperature, or a few hours on ice
PMSF is extremely destructive to the respriatory tract, eyes, and the skin; immediatly flush with copious amounts of water when exposed

recipe.name: Phleomycin
recipe.note: Working concentration is 100 microgram per milliliter for yeast
Purchase from WERNER BioAgents GmbH, Jena (Germany)

safety.disposal: Collect as HAZARDOUS WASTE. Avoid release to the environment.

recipe.name: Phosphate-buffered saline
recipe.note: Use between pH 5.8 to 8.0 unitless as buffer; commonly used at neutral pH
Forms insoluble precipitates with divalent cations; add dissolved magnesium or calcium stocks drop-wise to stirred 1 fold buffer
Phosphate inhibits reactions of phosphorylated metabolites, including enzymatic cleavage and ligation of DNA

recipe.name: Phosphatase inhibitor cocktail
recipe.workup: Add sodium orthovanadate drop-wise; if the solution turns brown, the orthovanadate polymerized, start over again
recipe.note: The cocktail inhibits several ATPases, protein tyrosine, acidic, or alkaline phosphatases, and other phosphate-transferring enzymes
Dithiothreitol (DTT) rapidly inactivates sodium orthovanadate

recipe.name: Poly-l-Lysine
recipe.workup: Use sterile liquids, the solution cannot be filter sterilized
recipe.note: Purchase from SERVA Electrophoresis (Germany)
Use 1 milliliter of a 0.1 gram per liter solution to coat a 25 square centimeter culture dish, apply for 5 minute, thoroughly rinse the surface, let dry for 120 minute

recipe.name: Potassium phosphate
recipe.workup: Adjust pH with potassium hydroxide if necessary
recipe.note: Use to prepare Gomori's phosphate buffers of different pH

recipe.name: Potassium phosphate
recipe.workup: Adjust pH with phosphoric acid if necessary
recipe.note: Use to prepare Gomori's phosphate buffers of different pH

recipe.name: Protease inhibitor cocktail (HALT)
recipe.note: The cocktail inhibits aminopeptidases, cysteine, serine, and aspartic acid proteases
Commercially available formulations are more cost-effective

recipe.name: Protease inhibitor cocktail
recipe.note: Commercially available

recipe.name: Puromycin
recipe.note: Working concentration is 1 to 10 microgram per milliliter for cell culture

safety.hazard: Kidney and liver toxicity upon repeated exposure

recipe.name: RNase A
recipe.workup: Heat to 100 celsius for 15 min to inactivate DNase contaminants; let cool to room temperature

recipe.name: S. O. C. medium
recipe.workup: Adjust pH with approximatley 20 milliliter 1 molar sodium hydroxide
Autoclave before adding sterile-filtered magnesium salts and glucose
recipe.note: S. O. C. is Super Optimal Broth (S. O. B.) with glucose added

recipe.name: Sodium azide
recipe.workup: Filter sterilize through a 0.45 micrometer filter
recipe.note: This stock is about 10 percent
Use at 0.1 percent final to inhibit the growth of bacteria and molds
Reacts with metal spatulas, metal equipment, pipes, traps, drains, and heavy metals to form SHOCK SENSITIVE salts

recipe.name: Sodium butyrate
recipe.workup: Add solids slowly into well stirred solution
recipe.note: Butyric acid is a corresive that smells like vomit or rancid butter; work inside a certified chemical fume hood

recipe.name: Sodium dodecyl sulfate
recipe.workup: Heat to 68 celsius to assist dissolution
Adjust pH to 7.2 with a few drops of concentrated hydrochloric acid
Filter sterilize through a 0.45 micrometer filter

recipe.name: Sodium hydroxide
recipe.workup: Add solids slowly into well stirred solution
recipe.note: The solution has 14.0
Work inside a chemical fume hood

recipe.name: Sodium orthovanadate
recipe.workup: Adjust to 10 with either 1 molar sodium hydroxide or 1 molar hydrochloric acid, depending on source; the solution will be yellow
Boil for 5 to 10 minute until colorless
Cool to room temperature and readjust to 10
Repeat until the solution remains colorless at 10
recipe.note: Depolymerization converts vanadate in a more potent inhibitor of protein tyrosine phosphatases
Dithiothreitol (DTT) rapidly inactivates orthovanadate

recipe.name: Sodium phosphate
recipe.workup: Adjust pH with sodium hydroxide if necessary
recipe.note: Use to prepare Gomori's phosphate buffers of different pH
Solubility of sodium phosphate in water is 100 gram per liter

recipe.name: Sodium phosphate
recipe.workup: Adjust pH with phosphoric acid if necessary
recipe.note: Use to prepare Gomori's phosphate buffers of different pH

recipe.name: Spectinomycin
recipe.note: Working concentration is 20 to 60 microgram per milliliter

recipe.name: Sucrose
recipe.workup: Heat to 37 celsius to fully dissolve
recipe.note: This stock is 85 percent sucrose

recipe.name: Synthetic dextrose (SD) minimal medium
recipe.note: SD is synthetic minimal medium containing salts, trace lements, vitamins, a nitrogen source, and glucose

recipe.name: Synthetic complete (SC) minimal medium
recipe.note: SC is SD to which various growth supplements are added such that auxotrophic strains can grow
Reduce the amount of SC drop-out mix according to the dropped-out components

recipe.name: Synthetic complete (SC) drop-out mix
recipe.workup: Mix powders thoroughly in a blender or shake for at least 15 minute
recipe.note: Other amino acid formulations, including CSM and HC, contain fewer ingredients since most laboratory strains are only auxotrophic for adenine, arginine, histidine, leucine, methionine, tryptophan, or uracil
Dry growth supplements can be premixed and stored for several years

recipe.name: Terrific broth (TB) medium
recipe.workup: Autoclave; cool to 60 celsius before adding phosphate buffers
recipe.note: TB is a rich growth medium to support high cell density in logarithmic phase for an extended period of time, resulting in higher yields of plasmid DNA or heterologous proteins

recipe.name: Tetracycline
recipe.note: Working concentration is 10 to 15 microgram per milliliter

recipe.name: Trichloroacetic acid
recipe.workup: Prepare directly in the TCA container
recipe.note: This stock is approximately 13.5 molar

recipe.name: Tris-borate-EDTA
recipe.storage: Do not store below 4 celsius to avoid precipitation of the stock.
recipe.note: Although substitution of disodium EDTA for the free acid will reduce electrical current and heat generated during electrophoresis, this is detrimental to the resolution of small DNA bands due to breakdown of buffering capacity

recipe.name: Tris hydrochloride
recipe.note: If stock appears yellow, discard and obtain better quality Tris
The pH of Tris buffers significantly changes with temperature
Use between pH 7.4 to 8.8 unitless as buffer

recipe.name: Tris hydrochloride
recipe.note: If stock appears yellow, discard and obtain better quality Tris
The pH of Tris buffers significantly changes with temperature
Use between pH 7.4 to 8.8 unitless as buffer

recipe.name: Triton™ X-100
recipe.workup: Filter sterilize through a 0.45 micrometer filter

recipe.name: Trypsin solution
recipe.workup: Use sterile liquids or filter sterilize
Dispense in 50 milliliter aliquots for routine use

recipe.name: Trypsin inhibitor solution
recipe.workup: Use sterile liquids or filter sterilize
recipe.note: The amount of soy inhibitor is directly related to the amount of trypsin it will inhibit; add an equal volume of trypsin inhibitor matching the concentration of the trypisin solution used for dissociation

recipe.name: Tween™ 20
recipe.note: Polysorbate 20 is prone to oxidation; if stock turns yellow, discard

recipe.name: X-Gal
recipe.note: Produces a blue precipitate upon hydrolysis

safety.hazard: Harmful if swallowed or in contact with skin

recipe.name: YEG medium
recipe.note: YEG is a complex medium to which additional carbon sources can be added; for most purposes, a final concentration of 2 percent sugar is sufficient
Bacto™ agar, Bacto™ peptone, and Bacto™ yeast extract are recommended
If raffinose is supplemented as carbon source, add the filter-sterilized 20 percent stock after the autoclaved medium has cooled down

recipe.name: YPD medium
recipe.note: YPD is a complex medium for routine growth of yeasts
Bacto™ agar, Bacto™ peptone, and Bacto™ yeast extract are recommended

recipe.name: YPG medium
recipe.note: YPG is a complex medium containing glycerol as a non-fermentable carbon source that does not support growth of ϱ– or pet mutants
Bacto™ agar, Bacto™ peptone, and Bacto™ yeast extract are recommended

recipe.name: YT medium
recipe.workup: Adjust pH with sodium hydroxide; autoclave
recipe.note: YT, or TY, is a rich growth medium optimized for growth and maintenance of filamentous phage such as M13
Do not dilute

recipe.name: Inhibitor cocktail

ingredient.name: Trichostatin A
ingredient.note: HDAC class I (1, 2, 3) and class III (10, 11) inhibitor.

recipe.workup: Add or omit inhibitors as needed
recipe.note: This inhibitor cocktail preserves histone acetylation and phosphorylation marks
You can replace protease inhibitors (PMSF, Leupeptin, Pepstatin A, Aprotinin) with a commercial EDTA-free protease inhibitor cocktail

recipe.name: Swelling buffer
recipe.workup: Add DTT and inhibitor cocktail immediately before use

recipe.name: Sucrose buffer
recipe.workup: Add DTT and inhibitor cocktail immediately before use
recipe.note: Sucrose-containing solutions must be made up fresh on the day

recipe.name: Trypan blue solution
recipe.workup: Dissolve the dye in 80 percent of the final volume; bring to a slow boil, cool down and make up with water

recipe.name: Radioimmunoprecipitation lysis buffer
recipe.workup: Add reducing agent right before use
recipe.note: This is Proteintech’s optimized RIPA buffer recipe
IGEPAL® CA-630 is a mild detergent alternative to Nonidet P-40 which is no longer manufactured; it can be replaced with Triton™ X-100
Due to limited solubility, this buffer cannot be prepared as concentrated stock

recipe.name: NP-40 lysis buffer
recipe.workup: Add reducing agent right before use
recipe.note: Add up to 1 percent sodium deoxycholate to increase stringency
IGEPAL® CA-630 is a mild detergent alternative to Nonidet P-40 which is no longer manufactured; it can be replaced with Triton™ X-100

recipe.name: Tris-Triton buffer
recipe.workup: Add reducing agent right before use

recipe.name: Urea lysis buffer
recipe.workup: Reconstitute urea in a water bath of 25 to 30 celsius for 30 minute to aid dissolution
recipe.note: Do not warm above 30 celsius since isocyanates will form and irreversibly modify proteins
Prepare fresh, use within one day

recipe.name: Resuspension buffer
recipe.note: Add DTT and PMSF right before use

safety.hazard: Toxic if inhaled, ingested, or in contact with skin

recipe.name: Urea extraction buffer
recipe.workup: Reconstitute urea in 10 milliliter water before adding thiourea
Stir in a water bath of 25 to 30 celsius for 30 minute to aid dissolution
recipe.note: Do not warm above 30 celsius since isocyanates and isothiocyanates will form and irreversibly modify proteins
Prepare fresh, use within one day

recipe.name: Urea wash buffer
recipe.note: Do not warm above 30 celsius since isocyanates will form that are detrimental to proteins
Prepare fresh before use or store at -80 celsius in 50 milliliter aliquots
Do not re-freeze

safety.precaution: nitrile gloves are NOT recommended for work with acetonitrile

recipe.name: Unfolding buffer
recipe.note: Use ultrapure guanidine hydrochloride

recipe.name: Refolding buffer
recipe.workup: Add reducing agent right before use

recipe.name: Tris-EDTA-KCl
recipe.note: At 4 celsius, diluted TEK buffers will be at 7.8
Potassium chloride can be replaced with sodium chloride for mononucleosome reconstitution
For arrays, high potassium concentrations prevent nucleosome stacking and precipitation PMID:2719953,PMID:25688036

recipe.name: Laemmli sample buffer
recipe.workup: Add reducing agent freshly; dispense into 920 microliter aliquots and make up to 1 milliliter with 80 microliter 2-mercaptoethanol just before use. Alternatively, make up with water and add dithiothreitol to 100 millimolar when diluting samples
recipe.note: Bromophenol blue is saturated at 1.0 gram per liter
The high chloride concentration in this sample buffer aids stacking

recipe.name: Tris-Gly running buffer
recipe.note: Do not adjust even if above 9.0 since glycine (rather than chloride) must remain the trailing ion in the buffer

recipe.name: Bis-Tris
recipe.note: This buffer can be prepared at 6.5 to aid resolution
Bis-Tris strongly interacts with copper, lead, and other heavy metal cations

recipe.name: LDS sample buffer
recipe.note: 8 percent LDS can be substituted with up to 4 percent SDS.
Brilliant Blue G gives a sharper dye front in MES and MOPS running buffers and migrates more closely to the moving ion front than bromophenol blue
Phenol red will turn yellow when the running buffer was poorly made or recycled too many times
Commercialized in XT or NuPAGE® systems

recipe.name: Low molecular weight marker
recipe.workup: Dilute each components in 500 microliter 1 fold sample buffer
recipe.note: Check the integrity of each component on a gel before combining
Apply 20 microliter low molecular weight marker (LMW) per lane

recipe.name: High molecular weight marker
recipe.workup: Dilute each component in 500 microliter 1 fold sample buffer
recipe.note: Check the integrity of each component on a gel before combining
Apply 20 microliter high molecular weight marker (HMW) per lane

recipe.name: Tris-Gly transfer buffer (Bjerrum)
recipe.note: Use for native proteins

recipe.name: CAPS buffer
recipe.note: Use for basic proteins
Some protocols use 100 millimolar CAPS for protein crystallography or general biochemistry, but this is not typical for Westerns

recipe.name: Ponceau S solution
recipe.note: The 0.01 percent solution is as sensitive as a 0.1 percent solution

recipe.name: TBS with Tween™ 20
recipe.workup: Add sodium azide to 0.05 percent to increase shelflife
recipe.note: Compatible with PVDF or nitrocellulose (NC) membranes; do not use Tween™ 20 with nylon membranes

recipe.name: Coomassie destaining solution
recipe.workup: Add concentrated acid slowly to the stirred water/alcohol mixture
recipe.note: Work inside a certified chemical fume hood
In this adaptation, 30 percent isopropyl alcohol or ethanol replaces the more toxic 45 percent methanol
The destaining solution can be reused several times and refreshed using activated charcoal

recipe.name: Coomassie staining solution
recipe.note: Coomassie G-250 is more sensitive while Coomassie R-250 gives sharper bands at lower cost
The staining solution can be used multiple times

recipe.name: Sodium thiosulfate
recipe.note: This is a 630 millimolar stock

recipe.name: Silver nitrate developer
recipe.note: Add formaldehyde no more than one hour before use to ensure consistent developer performance

recipe.name: Potassium ferricyanide
recipe.note: This is a 150 millimolar stock solution

recipe.name: Silver nitrate destaining solution
recipe.note: The yellowish solution is only active for 30 minute; use immediately

recipe.name: Polyethyleneimine
recipe.workup: Bring the stirred solution to neutral pH
Be patient, this may take hours! Recheck pH after 15 minute to ensure it has not drifted
Filter-sterilize through a 0.22 micrometer polyethersulfone (PES) membrane to remove undissolved particles
recipe.note: Purchase PEI from Polysciences (Cat. no. 23966)

recipe.name: HEPES-buffered saline
recipe.note: The pH is extremely critical for a successful transfection; even 0.1 pH units deviation reduces efficiency

recipe.name: Insulin
recipe.workup: Use sterile liquids; allow 15 minute for reconstitution
recipe.note: Obtain insulin from Sigma-Aldrich® (Cat. no. I1882)

recipe.name: Hydrocortisone
recipe.note: Obtain hydrocortisone from Sigma-Aldrich® (Cat. no. H0888)

recipe.name: Cholera toxin
recipe.note: Obtain cholera toxin from Sigma-Aldrich® (Cat. no. C8052)

recipe.name: Epidermal growth factor
recipe.workup: Use sterile liquids; allow 15 minute for reconstitution
recipe.note: Obtain EGF from PeproTech® (Cat. no. AF-100-15)

recipe.name: PEG virus precipitation solution
recipe.workup: Adjust pH to 7.2 with sodium hydroxide if necessary
Filter sterilize through a 0.22 micrometer filter

recipe.name: Hexadimethrine bromide
recipe.workup: Filter sterilize through a 0.22 micrometer PES membrane
recipe.note: Discard after thawing

recipe.name: Buffer QG (Extraction buffer)
recipe.workup: Prepare Buffer QG in the bottle without weighing
Add 117 milliliter water per 100 gram guanidinium thiocyanate, bring to 65 celsius to dissolve

recipe.name: Solution D
recipe.workup: Prepare solution D in the manufacturer’s bottle without weighing to avoid unnecessary handling of the hazardous material
Add 117 milliliter water per 100 gram guanidinium thiocyanate (volume increase), bring to 65 celsius to dissolve; dispense into 14 milliliter aliquots
Add 100 microliter 2-mercaptoethanol per aliquot freshly before use

recipe.name: Sodium N-lauroyl sarcosinate
recipe.note: Sarcosyl does not precipitate and is often used in place of SDS.

recipe.name: Phenol
recipe.workup: Add 20 milliliter nuclease-free reagent-grade water, bring to 65 celsius, let cool
Repeat until a second aqueous phase appears, then aspirate off the aqueous phase
recipe.note: blub

safety.hazard: Acute dermal, oral and inhalation toxicity

recipe.name: Micrococcal nuclease
recipe.note: Nuclease activity requires 1 to 5 millimolar Ca^2^+ and less than 100 millimolar monovalent salt
Multiple freeze-thaw cycles are tolerated

recipe.name: Ethylene glycol tetraacetic acid
recipe.workup: EGTA will not dissolve well until 8; titrate in with NaOH

recipe.name: Nuclear extraction buffer, low-salt (LS)
recipe.note: The addition of detergent is optional, but facilitates resuspension of the chromatin fraction
To enhance protein complex stability for downstream IP, supplement the buffer with 10 to 20 percent glycerol
Replace Tris-Cl with HEPES if planning for downstream mass spectrometry

recipe.name: Nuclear extraction buffer, high-salt (HS)
recipe.note: The addition of detergent is optional, but facilitates resuspension of the chromatin fraction
To enhance protein complex stability for downstream IP, supplement the buffer with 10 to 20 percent glycerol
Replace Tris-Cl with HEPES if planning for downstream mass spectrometry

recipe.name: Formaldehyde
recipe.note: When starting from paraformaldehyde, bring to 80 celsius for 1 to 2 hour and filter through a 0.22 micrometer pore

safety.precaution: Handle formaldehyde in a chemical fume hood

recipe.name: General IP buffer
recipe.note: This IP buffer is recommended for cytosolic proteins and soluble nuclear factors
Replace Tris-Cl with HEPES when planning for mass spectrometry
Triton™ X-100 can be replaced with other non-ionic detergents such as IGEPAL® CA-630 as an alternative to Shell’s discontinued NP-40 to preserve native protein interactions

recipe.name: Nuclear IP buffer
recipe.note: Optimized for chromatin-associated or nucleosomal targets
Replace Tris-Cl with HEPES when planning for mass spectrometry

recipe.name: Triethanolamine buffer
recipe.workup: Adjust to 8.9 with hydrochloric acid or sodium hydroxide as needed

recipe.name: Acidic elution buffer
recipe.workup: Adjust the pH to 2.5 at 4 celsius

recipe.name: Neutralization buffer
recipe.workup: Adjust the pH to 10.4 at 4 celsius

recipe.name: Thymidine
recipe.note: Thymidine can be reused after freeze–thawing, but avoid multiple cycles

recipe.name: Nocodazole
recipe.note: This stock is 4 millimolar
Working concentration ranges 0.2 to 2.0 micromolar (50 to 500 nanogram per milliliter), depending on the cell type

recipe.name: Propidium iodide staining solution
recipe.note: RNA must be removed by RNase to avoid overestimation of DNA content

recipe.name: Borax
recipe.workup: Filter through 0.45 micrometer filter or autoclave
recipe.storage: Do not store below 4 celsius to avoid precipitation of the stock.

recipe.name: Lithium-acetate
recipe.workup: Filter through 0.45 micrometer pore or autoclave

recipe.name: Tris-borate
recipe.workup: Filter through 0.45 micrometer pore or autoclave
recipe.storage: Do not store below 4 celsius to avoid precipitation of the stock.

recipe.name: Gel loading buffer
recipe.workup: Omit SDS with SYBR® Safe or GelRed DNA staining dyes
recipe.note: Polysucrose (Ficoll®) in the loading buffer results in sharper bands than 10 percent glycerol- or 10 percent sucrose-based 1 fold loading buffers

recipe.name: Xylene cyanol FF
recipe.note: This is a saturated solution

recipe.name: Bromophenol blue
recipe.note: This is a saturated solution

recipe.name: Cresol red
recipe.note: This is a saturated solution

recipe.name: DNA ladder
recipe.note: Load 1 to 2 microgram (4 to 8 microliter) for a 5 millimeter wide gel lane; scale accordingly

recipe.name: MOPS-acetate running buffer
recipe.workup: Adjust the pH with 2 molar sodium hydroxide before adding EDTA

recipe.name: RF1
recipe.workup: Adjust pH with 0.2 molar acetic acid

recipe.name: RF2
recipe.workup: Adjust pH with 1 molar sodium hydroxide

recipe.name: Simple & efficient buffer
recipe.workup: Adjust pH with 5 molar potassium hydroxide before adding manganese chloride; filter sterilize
